你对治疗慢性疼痛的新疗法有何感想，这种疗法虽然比任何可用的替代疗法都有效得多，但也可能改变你的宗教信仰？或者淋巴瘤的治疗会让三分之一的患者得到缓解，但也会让他们更有可能投票给你最不喜欢的政党？
这些看起来像是关于不可能的副作用的空洞的假设问题。毕竟，这不是医学的作用方式。但是一种新的心理健康治疗，将于明年获得许可，就会带来这样的问题。在“神奇蘑菇”中的迷幻化合物裸盖菇素的辅助下进行的心理治疗，似乎在治疗广泛的精神疾病方面非常有效，但也引起了一系列在其他医学上看不到的不寻常的非临床变化。
虽然其确切的治疗机制尚不清楚，但临床相关剂量的裸盖菇素可以诱导强大的神秘体验，更多的是与长时间的禁食、祈祷或冥想有关。因此，可以说，它能给患者带来长期的变化也就不足为奇了：研究报告称，亲社会性和审美价值的增加，加上个性、价值观和生活态度的强劲转变，甚至导致一些无神论者找到了上帝。更重要的是，这些体验似乎是裸盖菇素辅助心理治疗的一个特征，而不是一个错误，神秘体验的强度与临床受益的程度相关。
这些无疑是有趣的发现，但其中有什么重要的吗？不管治疗的结果多么不寻常，难道我们不应该优先考虑知情的、同意的患者的偏好吗？是的，我知道这可能会以奇怪的方式改变我。但是我的忧郁症让我很虚弱。我要掷骰子。撇开一个人对这种激进变革的了解有多深的问题不谈，政治现实让事情变得更加复杂，裸盖菇素-目前是附表1，高度非法的药物-的案例生动地展示了价值观、政治和社会叙事如何影响生物医学科学的发展。
非法的禁忌并不是不可逾越的障碍。多学科迷幻研究协会(MAPS)，一个倡导“谨慎使用”致幻剂的组织，在将MDMA(即摇头丸)恢复为合法药物方面取得了令人印象深刻的进展。MAP的主线是集中展示其治疗创伤后应激障碍的潜力。通过阐明MDMA辅助治疗如何帮助退伍军人，对退伍军人的支持享有罕见的两党协议，地图吸引了来自各个政治派别的支持者，得到了MSNBC和福克斯新闻(Fox News)的积极报道。
裸盖菇素辅助治疗的倡导者将其吹捧为新兴的精神健康危机的解决方案。但是，和MDMA一样，裸盖菇素远不是一种文化中立的药物，它既带有附表1地位的耻辱，也带有参差不齐的社会历史。它也可能需要建立MDMA辅助治疗享有的那种政治上不同的支持者联盟。
但要想获得广泛的吸引力，有一项挑战尤为突出：裸盖菇素似乎让人们变得更加自由。将迷幻使用和自由主义价值观联系起来的科学报告最早可以追溯到1971年，虽然这些发现在最近得到了重复，但一个非因果的解释很容易得到。那些持保守态度的人倾向于对非法药物使用持更不赞成的态度，这使得他们一开始就不太可能尝试迷幻药物。
然而，新出现的证据表明，这种关系可能是因果的，临床使用的裸盖菇素积极地改变了政治价值观，就像它改变了许多其他非临床特征一样。值得注意的是，一项关于裸盖菇素治疗难治性抑郁症的研究报告说，这种治疗减少了患者中的威权政治观点。这项临床试验还检测到了之前在健康参与者中报道的另一种影响：裸盖菇素的使用导致开放个性领域的增加，而开放本身就是自由主义价值观的预测指标。
如果裸盖菇素确实改变了政治价值观，这种影响的意义将比政治家或媒体寻求支持或阻碍裸盖菇素辅助治疗的意义更深。关于这个世俗民主国家的一个公认的共识是，它应该在一些问题上保持中立和不可知论，允许公民的价值观、政治态度和宗教信仰的多样性。在这些州拥有全民医疗体系的地方，不仅允许支持，而且允许通过纳税人的捐款为这种向一个方向转变价值观的治疗提供资金吗？
由于样本量目前很小，需要更多的研究来了解是否真的存在因果关系，如果是的话，其性质可能是什么。也许裸盖菇素并不能诱导自由主义价值观，而是巩固了治疗前存在的任何价值观。一种根深蒂固的医疗保健模式至少不太可能树敌。对于沿着政治光谱将患者转移到一个方向的治疗就不能说同样的话了。
为了克服这一障碍，裸盖菇素辅助治疗的倡导者需要一面鼓舞人心的旗帜，任何政治部落的成员都可以团结在它周围。由于几乎没有什么东西能像政治那样强大地将我们团结在一起，也许最具诱惑力的旗帜将是将我们所有人团结在一起的一件事：死亡。虽然裸盖菇素既不能治愈死亡，也不能预防死亡，但研究一再表明，它可以在未来的姑息治疗中发挥深远的作用。面对危及生命的疾病或绝症时所经历的生存痛苦，可能会夺走为垂死之人留下的微不足道的生活质量。这样的痛苦对我们的标准药物方法反应不佳，但裸盖菇素诱导的强大的神秘体验不断地将士气低落、焦虑和抑郁转化为接受、平静和意义，因为患者准备迎接他们的死亡。
无论他们在其他方面有何不同，保守派和自由派都团结在一起，知道他们和他们所爱的人最终都会死去。对于保守派和自由派来说，裸盖菇素可以帮助他们以更大的接受度和更少的恐惧来迎接这一结局。裸盖菇素看起来将在2022年成为特许药物。但最终有多少人从中受益，这不仅是它的效果如何的问题，也是它到来时围绕它的叙述的问题：裸盖菇素是否强调了我们是如何不同，还是我们是如何相同的？
note_en:
How would you feel about a new therapy for your chronic pain, which—although far more effective than any available alternative—might also change your religious beliefs? Or a treatment for lymphoma that brings one in three patients into remission, but also made them more likely to vote for your least preferred political party?
 These seem like idle hypothetical questions about impossible side effects. After all, this is not how medicine works. But a new mental health treatment, set to be licensed next year, poses just this sort of problem. Psychotherapy assisted by psilocybin, the psychedelic compound in “magic mushrooms,” seems to be remarkably effective in treating a wide range of psychopathologies, but also causes a raft of unusual nonclinical changes not seen elsewhere in medicine.
 Although its precise therapeutic mechanisms remain unclear, clinically relevant doses of psilocybin can induce  powerful mystical experiences more commonly associated with extended periods of fasting, prayer or meditation. Arguably, then, it is unsurprising that it can generate long-lasting changes in patients: studies report increased prosociality and aesthetic appreciation, plus robust shifts in  personality,  values and  attitudes to life, even  leading some atheists to find God. What’s more, these experiences appear to be a feature, rather than a bug, of psilocybin-assisted psychotherapy, with the  intensity of the mystical experience correlating with the extent of clinical benefit.
 These are undoubtedly interesting findings, but should any of it matter? However unusual a treatment’s consequences, shouldn’t we prioritize the preferences of an informed, consenting patient?  Yes, I understand that this might change me in strange ways. But my depression is debilitating. I will roll that dice. Putting aside the matter of how well-informed one could really be about such radical transformations, political realities make things more complicated, with the case of psilocybin— currently a Schedule 1, highly illicit drug—showing vividly how values, politics and social narratives can influence the development of biomedical science.
 The taboo of the illicit is not an insuperable obstacle. The Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies (MAPS), an organization that advocates for “careful uses” of psychedelics, has gone an impressive way in rehabilitating MDMA (i.e., ecstasy) into a legitimate medicine. MAPS’s masterstroke was to focus on demonstrating its potential for treating PTSD. By articulating how MDMA-assisted therapy could help veterans, support for whom enjoys a rare level of bipartisan agreement, MAPS have attracted supporters from across the political spectrum, receiving positive coverage from MSNBC and Fox News alike.
 Advocates of psilocybin-assisted therapy tout it as the solution to the burgeoning mental health crisis. But, like MDMA, psilocybin is far from a culturally neutral drug, carrying both the shame of Schedule 1 status and a checkered social history. It too may need to build the kind of politically heterogeneous coalition of supporters that MDMA-assisted therapy enjoys.
 But to generate a breadth of appeal, one challenge stands out: psilocybin seems to make people more liberal. Scientific reports associating psychedelic use and liberal values stretch back as far as 1971, and although these findings have been replicated more recently, a noncausal explanation is readily available. Those with conservative attitudes tend to look more disapprovingly on illicit drug use, making them less likely than liberals to try a psychedelic drug in the first place.
 However, emerging evidence suggests the relationship could be causal, with clinically administered psilocybin actively shifting political values, just as it shifts many other nonclinical characteristics. Notably, one study of psilocybin for treatment-resistant depression reported that the treatment  decreased authoritarian political views in patients. That clinical trial also detected another effect that had previously been reported in healthy participants:  psilocybin use leads to increases in the personality domain of openness, itself a predictor of liberal values.
 If psilocybin does change political values, the significance of this effect goes deeper than which politicians or media outlets will seek to support or impede psilocybin-assisted therapy. A well-established consensus on the secular democratic state is that it should remain neutral and agnostic on a number of matters, allowing a diversity of values, political attitudes and religious beliefs among its citizens. Where such states have universal health care systems, is it permissible to not only endorse, but fund through taxpayer contributions, a treatment which shifts values in one direction?
 With sample sizes currently small, more research is needed to understand whether there truly is a causal relationship at work, and, if so, what its nature might be. Perhaps psilocybin doesn’t so much induce liberal values, but rather consolidates whatever values were present before treatment. A health care modality that entrenches preexisting political sentiments is, at the least, unlikely to make enemies. The same could not be said of a treatment that shifts patients in one direction along the political spectrum.
 To overcome this obstacle, advocates of psilocybin-assisted therapy need an inspiring banner that members of any political tribe could rally around. With few things that unite us as powerfully as politics can divide us, perhaps the most alluring banner will be the one thing that unites us all: death. While psilocybin is neither a cure for, nor a prophylactic against, death, studies  have  repeatedly  demonstrated that it could play a profound role in the future of palliative care. The existential distress experienced when faced with a life-threatening or terminal illness can steal away what little quality of life remains for the dying. Such distress responds poorly to our standard pharmaceutical approaches, but the powerful mystical experiences induced by psilocybin consistently transmute demoralization, anxiety and depression into acceptance, peacefulness and meaning, as patients prepare to meet their death.
 However else they differ, conservatives and liberals are united in knowing that they, and their loved ones, will eventually die. And for conservatives and liberals alike, psilocybin could help them welcome the end with greater acceptance and less fear. Psilocybin looks set to become a licensed medicine by 2022. But how many ultimately benefit from it will be a matter not just of how well it works, but also the narrative surrounding it when it arrives: does psilocybin underline how we are different, or how we are the same?
title: 如果一粒药丸就能改变你的政治或宗教信仰呢？
title_en: What if a Pill Can Change Your Politics or Religious Beliefs?

note:
1975年，加州大学洛杉矶分校的精神病学家罗伯特·斯托勒(Robert Stoller)在他的教科书中写下了一些关于性和性别的离奇东西。他断言，那些出生时被认为是男孩，但性别身份或表情与这一假设不符的人“通常有漂亮的脸，有漂亮的头发，可爱的皮肤，优雅的动作，特别是--大而锐利的眼睛。”基于这一观察，他提出了一个理论模型，在这个模型中，变性人女孩变成变性人是因为她们特别可爱。他推断，社会对待她们更像是女孩，因为这段经历，她们开始认同为女性。
作为一名内科科学家，我一般认为知识会带来进步。但围绕这一特定问题的研究--那些询问是什么决定了一个人的性别认同的研究--引导我们走上了一些奇怪而危险的道路。这一领域的研究人员似乎在寻找一些客观事实，但这门科学植根于一个主观假设：我们需要知道是什么让一个人成为跨性别者，这样他们才能被“固定”。因此，科学家们不遗余力地追寻这样的问题，发起了一些研究，宣传可能会伤害变性人儿童及其家庭的想法。
斯托勒的观察激发了人们变性背后的许多心理学理论。1993年，多伦多克拉克精神病学研究所的一组研究人员着手测试他的假设，即美貌和当时被称为“性别认同障碍”的疾病是有联系的。他们招募了17名有诊断的出生分配男孩和17名没有诊断的出生分配男孩，他们都在8岁左右。研究人员随后拍摄了这些孩子的头部照片，并将其展示给36名大学生。学生们被要求给这些年轻人的外貌打分，从1分到5分，类别包括“有吸引力的”、“英俊的”和“漂亮的”。最终，大学生发现患有“性别认同障碍”的孩子比顺性男孩“更漂亮”。这些发现似乎表明斯托勒是对的：也许是因为他们的外表，人们把前一组中的年轻人更像女孩一样对待，结果他们变成了变性人。尽管如论文后面提到的，一个同样可信的理论是，这些孩子可能改变了他们的外表(长发等)，使大学生们将他们与更多的女性描述联系在一起，比如“漂亮”。
几年后，研究人员恢复了这一调查路线，使用了患有“性别认同障碍”的年轻出生分配女孩的头像。与顺性女孩相比，一群大学生再次对这些孩子的外表进行了“丑陋”或“漂亮”的评分。患有“性别认同障碍”的儿童被评为不那么漂亮，这促使研究人员提出，他们可能受到了更多的男孩对待，因此被认定为男性。似乎更有可能的是，这些孩子只是把头发剪短了，所以参与者给他们附加了更多的男性词汇。最后，这项研究并没有太多地揭示是什么让一个人成为变性人，但它确实促进了一种攻击性的理论，可能会削弱脆弱的变性人青年的自尊。
研究人员还研究了这些孩子的父母。长期以来，精神病学一直痴迷于母亲损害孩子发育的理论(例如，冰箱母亲理论假设自闭症是由于缺乏母亲的温暖所致)。这些研究同样询问是否父母应该为他们孩子的性别认同“负责”。在一篇论文中，研究人员评估了患有“性别认同障碍”儿童的母亲是否有更多的抑郁症状或一种称为边缘性人格障碍的疾病。他们发现这些母亲都有更多的这两种症状。听起来很有说服力，对吧？孩子们必须成为变性人，因为他们的母亲患有精神疾病。
研究人员没有讨论的是，母亲的症状很容易由社会对待孩子的方式引起。边缘型人格障碍的分量表较高的是“人际冲突”。即使你不是变性人孩子的父母，你也能想象在一个不被接受的社区里抚养你的孩子可能会造成实质性的冲突。
在另一项研究中，研究人员指出，患有“性别认同障碍”儿童的父母没有对刻板印象中的性别非典型行为施加强有力的限制，比如出生的男孩玩洋娃娃，出生的女孩玩积木或交通玩具。也许这就是“问题”的原因？作者推测，如果这些父母在早期就简单地打击这种行为-比方说，从他们蹒跚学步的孩子手中夺走芭比娃娃-他们可能已经阻止了这种行为。解释的可能性越大
这些关于性别认同起源的理论只会增加被误导的、越来越非法的呼吁，要求为变性人提供顺性别化的“疗法”。所谓性别认同转换疗法的逻辑是，如果环境是原因，那么我们可以简单地改变环境，将事情扼杀在萌芽状态。大多数“皈依”手册还没有向公众发布，但在2002年，哥伦比亚大学的一位心理学家出版了“年轻男孩中的性别认同障碍：基于父母和同伴的协议”，其中包括一些育儿技巧，如“母亲放过男孩”，强迫孩子与同性朋友玩耍，以及将青少年从体操或芭蕾舞等刻板印象中的非典型性别活动中剔除。值得注意的是，我和我的同事最近进行的一项研究表明，试图将儿童的性别身份从变性人改变为顺性人与自杀未遂的可能性更大相关。美国有几个州已经禁止转换疗法，但在美国大部分地区，这种做法仍在继续。
即使在今天，对变性人身份的心理原因的类似研究仍在继续。布朗大学(Brown University)的一位医生最近对通过网站招募的受访者进行了一项匿名调查，调查对象是那些认为同龄人压力和网络影响让他们的孩子变性人的父母。这项调查基本上是询问父母是否认为互联网让他们的孩子变性人，考虑到他们访问网站了解这个想法，父母们毫不奇怪地回答是的。保守派媒体抓住了这项研究，认为跨性别者儿童真的只是在Reddit上读到一些东西后被骗成为跨性别者的困惑的孩子。言下之意是，我们需要把这些孩子从支持LGBTQ的在线社区中带走，这样他们就可以再次成为顺性别。通读这些文献，我们需要问自己一些问题：为什么要进行这项研究？它希望实现什么目标？不知疲倦的寻找揭示了一个几乎不加掩饰的教条：变性人是一种有待修复的病理。这种信念不仅伤害了变性人，也破坏了好的科学。
好的科学告诉我们，当我们接纳变性人时，他们就会茁壮成长。我们不应该试图找出哪里出了问题，而应该把我们的时间和精力投入到倡导非歧视法律，增加获得医疗保健的机会，并在媒体上提高跨性别者的声音，这样社会才能意识到他们是我们社区的重要成员。当斯托勒注意到这些孩子是特殊的时候，也许他是对的。现在是我们庆祝并继续前进的时候了。
你是专门研究神经科学、认知科学或心理学的科学家吗？你有没有读过最近一篇你想写的同行评议论文？请将建议发送给Mind Matters编辑加雷斯·库克(Gareth Cook)。加雷斯是普利策奖获奖记者，是最佳美国信息图表的系列编辑，可以通过gmail.com或twitter@garthdeas联系到他。
note_en:
In 1975 psychiatrist Robert Stoller of the University of California, Los Angeles, wrote something bizarre in his  textbook on sex and gender. He asserted that people who were assumed to be boys when they were born but whose gender identity or expression did not match that assumption “often  have pretty faces, with fine hair, lovely complexions, graceful movements, and—especially—big, piercing, liquid eyes.”  Based on this observation, he suggested  a theoretical model in which transgender girls become transgender because they are especially cute. Society treats them more like girls, he reasoned, and because of this experience, they start to identify as female.
  As a physician-scientist, I’m generally of the opinion that knowledge leads to progress. But studies focused on this particular question—those asking what determines someone’s gender identity—have led us down some strange and dangerous paths. Researchers in this area appear to be in search of some objective truth, but the science is rooted in a subjective assumption: that we need to know what makes someone transgender so that they can be “fixed.” As a result, scientists have relentlessly pursued such questions, launching studies that promoted ideas that could hurt transgender children and their families.
  Stoller’s observations motivated many of the psychological theories behind what makes people transgender. In 1993 a group of researchers at the Clarke Institute of Psychiatry in Toronto  set out to test his hypothesis that beauty and what was then called “gender identity disorder”  were linked. They recruited 17 birth-assigned boys with the diagnosis and 17 birth-assigned boys without it ,  all around the age of eight. The researchers then took headshots of the children and showed them to 36 college students. The students were asked to rate the youngsters’ physical appearance on a scale from one to five with categories such as “attractive,” “handsome” and “beautiful.” In the end, the college students found the children with “gender identity disorder” to be “prettier” than the cisgender boys. The findings seem to suggest Stoller was right: perhaps, because of their appearance, people treated the youngsters in the former group more like girls, and consequently, they became transgender. Though as the authors mention later in the paper, an equally plausible theory is that these children could have altered their appearance (long hair, et cetera) in ways that matched their identity, leading the college students to associate them with more feminine descriptions such as “pretty.”
  A few years later, researchers  revived this line of investigation, using the headshots of young birth-assigned girls with “gender identity disorder.” A group of college students again rated how “ugly” or “pretty” these children appeared, compared with cisgender girls. The children with “gender identity disorder” were rated as less beautiful, prompting the researchers to suggest that they may have been treated more like boys and thus identified as male. It seems more likely that these children simply cut their hair shorter, so the participants attached more masculine words to them. In the end, the study didn’t reveal much about what makes someone transgender, but it did promote an offensive theory with the potential to diminish the self-esteem of vulnerable transgender youth.
  Researchers also studied the parents of such children. Psychiatry has long been enamored with the theory of mothers harming the development of their children (for example, the  refrigerator mother theory posited that autism was caused by a lack of maternal warmth). These studies similarly asked if perhaps parents were to “blame” for their kids’ gender identity. In one paper, researchers assessed whether  the mothers of children with “gender identity disorder” had more symptoms of either depression or a condition called borderline personality disorder. They found these mothers had more symptoms of both. Sounds convincing, right? Children must become transgender because their mothers are mentally ill.
  What the researchers failed to discuss was that the mothers’ symptoms could easily have been caused by the way society treated their children. The subscale of borderline personality disorder  that was higher among them was “interpersonal conflict.” You don’t need to be the parent of a transgender child to imagine that raising your kid in an unaccepting community could create substantial conflict.
  In another study, researchers noted that parents of children with “gender identity disorder” did not place strong limits on stereotypically gender atypical behaviors such as birth-assigned boys playing with dolls or birth-assigned girls playing with blocks or transportation toys. Perhaps this was the cause of the “problem”? If these parents had simply cracked down on this behavior early on—ripped the Barbie out of their toddler’s hands, say—they may have prevented it, the authors posited. The more likely explanation is that it’s difficult to take a doll away from a child who desperately wants to play with it. And that doing so makes them sad and impacts their self-esteem.
  In each case, researchers were hyperfocused on finding a problem with either the kids or their parents. But in the end, these scientists failed to establish one. They seemed less interested in a vital reframing: perhaps the issue was not the children’s identity but the way society treated them. Instead of supporting these kids, the researchers labeled them unattractive or painted their parents as mentally unstable.
  These theories on the origins of gender identity have only added to the misguided, and  increasingly illegal, calls for “therapies” designed to make transgender people cisgender. The logic of so-called gender identity conversion therapy is  that if the environment is the cause, then we can simply alter the environment to nip things in the bud. Most of the “conversion” manuals have not been released to the public, but in 2002 a psychologist at Columbia University published “ Gender Identity Disorder in Young Boys: A Parent and Peer-Based Protocol,” which included parenting techniques such as “letting go of [the] boy by [the] mother,”  forcing the child to play with same-sex friends, and removing the youngster from stereotypically gender-atypical activities such as gymnastics or ballet. Notably,  a recent study my colleagues and I conducted showed that attempts to change a child’s gender identity from transgender to cisgender are associated with greater odds of attempting suicide. Several U.S. states have banned conversion therapy, but in much of the U.S., these practices continue.
  Similar research into the psychological causes of transgender identity continues even today. A physician at Brown University recently conducted an anonymous survey of respondents recruited via Web sites for parents who believe peer pressure and online influences have made their children transgender.  The survey essentially asked the parents if they thought the Internet made their children trans, and the parents, not surprisingly, given that they were visiting Web sites about this idea, answered yes. Conservative media latched onto the study, suggesting that transgender children are really just confused kids tricked into being transgender after reading something on Reddit. The implication is that we need to take these kids away from supportive online LGBTQ communities so that they can be made cisgender again. Reading through this literature, we need to ask ourselves some questions: What is the reason for this research? What does it hope to accomplish? The tireless search reveals a thinly veiled dogma: that being transgender is a pathology to be fixed. This belief not only harms transgender people but also undermines good science.
  What good science shows us is that when we accept transgender people,  they thrive. Instead of trying to figure out what went “wrong,” we should be investing our time and energy into advocating for nondiscrimination laws, increasing access to health care and raising transgender voices in the media, so society realizes that they are vital members of our communities. Maybe Stoller was right when he noted that those children were exceptional. It’s time we celebrate that and move on.
  Are you a scientist who specializes in neuroscience, cognitive science, or psychology? And have you read a recent peer-reviewed paper that you would like to write about? Please send suggestions to Mind Matters editor   Gareth Cook . Gareth, a Pulitzer prize-winning journalist, is the series editor of   Best American Infographics  and can be reached at garethideas AT gmail.com or Twitter @garethideas.
title: 变性人身份研究令人不安的历史
title_en: The Disturbing History of Research into Transgender Identity

note:
周一，世界卫生组织(World Health Organization)的高级官员在面对白宫幕僚长马克·梅多斯(Mark Meadows)暗示美国已经放弃控制大流行冠状病毒SARS-CoV-2传播的言论时，有时似乎感到愤怒、目瞪口呆和疲惫。
梅多斯周日在接受美国有线电视新闻网(CNN)采访时说：“我们无法控制这场大流行。”相反，我们将把重点放在“疫苗、治疗和其他缓解领域”，他说。这些言论引发了广泛的轩然大波，梅多斯周一试图平息这场轩然大波。但他的澄清只会强化他早先的言论。
“我的意思是，当我们看到这一点时，我们将击败病毒。我们无法控制它。我们将尽最大努力遏制它，“他昨天在白宫外对记者表示。他再次强调了治疗和疫苗的必要性。
在世卫组织周一的新闻发布会上，世卫组织总干事Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus直言不讳地说：“放弃控制是危险的。”
虽然Ghebreyesus同意治疗学和疫苗的重要性，但他指出，到目前为止，它们还没有推出。因此，“控制也应该是战略的一部分，”他说。“政府应该尽到自己的一份力量，公民也应该尽到自己的一份力量。否则，这种病毒是危险的。如果放任不管，可能会造成严重破坏。“
note_en:
Top officials at the World Health Organization on Monday appeared at times exasperated, flabbergasted, and wearied as they confronted comments by White House Chief of Staff Mark Meadows suggesting that the United States has given up trying to control the spread of the pandemic coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2.
 “We’re not going to control the pandemic,” Meadows said in  a CNN interview Sunday. Instead we will focus on “vaccines, therapeutics, and other mitigation areas,” he said. The comments spurred widespread uproar, which Meadows tried to quell Monday. But his clarification only reinforced his earlier comments.
 “I mean, when we look at this, we’re going to defeat the virus. We’re not going to control it. We will try to contain it as best we can,”  he told reporters outside the White House yesterday. He again emphasized the need for therapeutics and vaccines.
  In a WHO press briefing Monday, WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus spoke bluntly: “ Giving up on control is dangerous.”
 While Ghebreyesus agreed on the importance of therapeutics and a vaccine, he noted that so far, they are not yet available. As such, “control should also be part of the strategy,” he said. “The government should do its share and the citizens also should do their share. Otherwise, this virus is dangerous. If it’s let go freely, it can create havoc.”
title: 特朗普政府的大流行投降招致世卫组织的热情回应
title_en: Trump admin’s pandemic surrender draws impassioned response from WHO

像Groovy Gorilla这样的临时版本至少支持9个月-这意味着如果您正在进行快速发布，您将需要计划每6个月进行一次操作系统就地升级。
如果这对你来说太快了，4月份发布的偶数年版本是LTS(长期支持)版本，至少支持5年。
上周，Canonical发布了最新的Ubuntu中间版本，20.10；Groovy Gorilla&34；这是第一次为Raspberry Pi 4添加了一流的平台支持。
note_en:
Interim releases like Groovy Gorilla are supported for "at least 9 months"—meaning if you're on the fast-release track, you'll need to plan for an in-place OS upgrade every six months.
 If that's too fast for you, the April releases on even-numbered years are LTS (Long Term Support) versions, which get supported for at least five years.
Last week, Canonical released the latest intermediate version of Ubuntu, 20.10 "Groovy Gorilla"—which, for the first time, adds first-class platform support for the Raspberry Pi 4.
title: Ubuntu Groovy Gorilla将Raspberry Pi添加为“一等公民”
title_en: Ubuntu Groovy Gorilla adds Raspberry Pi as a “first class citizen”

note:
今天的Dealmaster应该会让市场上的任何人对新的任天堂Switch游戏板感兴趣，因为Switch Pro Controller和一对默认的Joy-Con游戏机控制器目前都便宜10美元。这两笔交易使它们分别降至59美元和69美元。这两个报价都不代表我们见过的绝对最低价，而且我们总是有可能在黑色星期五看到更大的降幅，但今年这两种产品的折扣都很少。对于Switch Pro Controller来说，这是我们在2020年看到的(在少数情况下)最好的价格。对于Joy-Cons来说，这是自1月份以来我们追踪到的最好的价格，尽管对于一些较新的变种来说，它与我们总体上看到的最大折扣相当。
至于设备本身，我们仍然推荐Switch Pro Controller给那些花在电视上玩开关的时间与在控制台的手持模式下一样长的人。它立即比默认的Joy-Cons更舒适，有更宽敞的触发器，面部按钮和操纵杆，一个真正的D-pad，以及持续约40小时的出色电池续航时间。它没有耳机插孔，但它支持任天堂的高清隆隆声、动作控制和amibo扫描功能。对于最近几年玩过Xbox或PlayStation的任何人来说，这个形状都应该有宾至如归的感觉，如果需要，它可以与游戏PC配合使用。
Joy-Cons不符合人体工程学，特别是在较长的游戏时间内，而且随着时间的推移，总是有漂移的风险。如果你只需要一个控制器，请注意任天堂将在未来几周内将Single Joy-Cons的价格降至40美元。但任天堂在解决任何可能出现的耐用性问题方面已经做得更好了，而且实际上，对于大多数玩远离大屏幕的游戏的玩家来说，没有太多的其他选择。
不过，如果你已经准备好在游戏手柄方面下功夫，那么今天的一系列优惠活动仍然有很多值得注意的优惠，包括苹果的AirPods Pro回落至迄今为止的最低价，我们最喜欢的开关保护套的大幅折扣，几款售价40美元的第一方开关游戏，最新联想ThinkPad X1碳素笔记本电脑的新低，外部存储的各种折扣，等等。你可以看看我们下面的完整摘要。
note_en:
Today's Dealmaster should be of interest to anyone in the market for a new Nintendo Switch gamepad, as both the  Switch Pro Controller and pairs of the console's  default Joy-Con controllers are currently $10 off. The deals bring them down to $59 and $69, respectively. Neither offer represents the  absolute lowest price we've seen, and there's always a chance we see steeper drops on Black Friday, but discounts on both products have been rare this year. For the Switch Pro Controller, this matches the best price we've seen (on a handful of occasions) in 2020. For the Joy-Cons, it's the best price we've tracked since January, though for  some newer variants it matches the biggest discount we've seen in general.
 As for the devices themselves, we  still recommend the Switch Pro Controller for anyone who spends as much time playing their Switch on the TV as they do in the console's handheld mode. It's immediately more comfortable than the default Joy-Cons, with roomier triggers, face buttons and joy-sticks, an actual d-pad, and excellent battery life that lasts around 40 hours. It doesn't have a headphone jack, but it does support Nintendo's HD rumble, motion control, and  amiibo scanning features. The shape should feel right at home for anyone who has played an Xbox or PlayStation in recent years, and it can work with a gaming PC if needed.
 The Joy-Cons aren't as ergonomic, especially over longer play sessions, and there's always the  risk of "drift" settling in over time. If you only need one controller, note that Nintendo will  soon drop the price of single Joy-Cons to $40 in the coming weeks. But Nintendo has  gotten better about  addressing any durability issues that may arise, and really, there aren't many other options for gamers who mostly play their Switch away from the big screen.
 If you're all set on the gamepad front, though, today's roundup of deals still has plenty of noteworthy offers, including Apple's AirPods Pro back down to their lowest price to date, a nice discount on one of our favorite Switch cases, several first-party Switch games for $40, a new low on the latest Lenovo ThinkPad X1 Carbon laptop, various discounts on external storage, and more. You can take a look at our full rundown below.
title: 任天堂的Switch Pro Controller和Joy-Cons今天都便宜10美元
title_en: Nintendo’s Switch Pro Controller and Joy-Cons are both $10 off today
